pattern: Halving is forced when even; when odd, choosing plus-one versus minus-one comes down to which removes more low-order one-bits, so the greedy bit rule matches the true minimum.
hints: Even numbers have only one sensible move; the real decision is which way to nudge an odd number.
Going toward a value with more trailing zeros lets you halve repeatedly, so prefer the choice that clears more low bits.
For odd n (except 3), add one when the second-lowest bit is set (n & 2), otherwise subtract one; 3 is the special case where subtracting wins.

edge_inputs.description: power of two halves straight down
already a small power of two
the special case where minus-one beats plus-one
32-bit ceiling where plus-one would overflow naive code

stress.description: random n across the full positive 32-bit range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    return [rng.randint(1, max(1, size))]
stress.note: large odd values stress the plus-one overflow boundary and the greedy bit rule
